title: Umbilical cord care
executiveSummary: Care for the umbilical stump in newborns weighing 500 g to 2500 g (days 0–30) using a dry or antiseptic technique according to institutional protocol, maintaining surveillance for omphalitis, and avoiding the application of non-recommended substances.
body:
## Steps to perform

1. At birth: dry the cord with sterile gauze; place the umbilical clamp 2–3 cm from the base (or according to institutional devices); keep the stump exposed to air if utilizing the dry method.
2. Educate the family: do not cover the stump with the diaper (fold the diaper below the stump); do not pull or manipulate the stump; wash hands before and after care.
3. Perform hand hygiene (WHO Moment 1) before inspecting the cord.
4. Inspect at every diaper change and at least once per shift: look for a clean dry or moist base, without erythema, foul odor, or purulent secretion.
5. If institutional protocol indicates the dry method (WHO recommended): do not apply alcohol, povidone-iodine, or ointments; allow the clamp to fall off when the stump is dry (usually 3–7 days).
6. If protocol indicates an antiseptic method: apply chlorhexidine 4% or 7.1% digluconate once a day to the base of the cord using a swab; apply gentle friction; allow to air dry.
7. Keep the area clean and dry; if contaminated with urine or feces, clean with warm water and neutral soap; dry completely.
8. Record the appearance of the cord on the neonatal chart: moist/dry, erythema, secretion, and day of stump separation.
9. Do not submerge the newborn in a bath until the stump falls off and healing is complete (use a sponge bath until then).
10. In case of signs of infection: check axillary temperature, and obtain a complete blood count (CBC) and culture as prescribed; do not remove the clamp during active omphalitis without formal evaluation.
11. If stump bleeding occurs: compress the base for 5–10 minutes with sterile gauze; if it persists, active ligation must be performed by trained personnel.
12. Document the day of cord separation; continue surveillance of the umbilical scar for 48 hours post-separation.

## Scientific rationale

### Population and scope

**Newborns day 0–30**, **500 g–2500 g**. Umbilical cord care is a preventive measure; omphalitis can progress to neonatal sepsis within hours, especially in preterm infants and resource-limited settings.

### Objective

- Promote safe drying and detachment of the stump (3–14 days).
- Prevent omphalitis and neonatal sepsis.
- Educate primary caregivers.

### Evidence and recommendations

> **Source:** WHO — Dry and clean umbilical cord care is recommended in settings with low infection rates; 7.1% chlorhexidine reduces omphalitis and mortality in high-risk settings (WHO, 2024).

> **Source:** AAP/ACOG — Do not apply non-sterile substances (talcum powder, powders, herbs); keep the cord visible and dry (American Academy of Pediatrics).

### Comparison of care methods

| Method | Clinical indication | Action |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Dry and clean | Units with low infection rates | No antiseptic; open to air |
| Chlorhexidine 4–7.1% | High risk of omphalitis / preterm infants | Daily application to the base |
| Alcohol 70% | Institutional alternative | 1–2 times/day if protocol indicates |

Do not mix methods without a direct indication; follow a uniform institutional protocol.

### Cord clamping

| Clinical situation | Clamping time |
| --- | --- |
| Vigorous term newborn | ≥ 30–60 s or until cessation of pulsations |
| < 34 weeks without resuscitation | Delayed clamping for 30–60 s if stable |
| Active resuscitation | Immediate clamping if necessary for access |

### Signs of omphalitis — notify immediately

| Grade | Findings | Action |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Mild | Erythema ≤ 2 cm at the base | Close surveillance; culture if fever occurs |
| Moderate | Erythema > 2 cm, exudate, odor | IV antibiotics; blood culture |
| Severe | Periumbilical cellulitis, necrosis | Sepsis management; ICU; surgery if necrosis |

### Normal detachment time

| Weight / GA | Days until separation |
| --- | --- |
| Preterm < 1000 g | 10–14 days |
| 1000–2500 g | 7–10 days |
| Term | 5–7 days |

A delay > 14 days or the presence of an umbilical granuloma requires evaluation by a neonatologist.

### Complications — notify immediately

- Omphalitis (erythema, warmth, purulent exudate, odor).
- Active bleeding from the stump not controlled by compression.
- Fever or hypothermia associated with cord changes.
- Persistent bleeding from an umbilical granuloma.
- Incarcerated umbilical hernia (rare in newborns; evaluate if a painful protrusion is present).

bibliography.citation: World Health Organization. Care of the umbilical cord — recommendations on newborn health.
World Health Organization. WHO recommendations on umbilical cord care for newborns.
American Academy of Pediatrics. Umbilical cord care in the newborn.
Vermont Oxford Network. Infection prevention bundles in the NICU and newborn nursery.
SATI — Prevention of neonatal infections and umbilical cord care.
